skill.name: adverse-event-narrative
skill.description: Generates CIOMS-compliant adverse event narratives for ICSR submissions to FDA, EMA, and health authorities from structured case data.
skill.summary: Regulatory-grade clinical narrative generator that transforms adverse event case data into CIOMS-compliant ICSR narratives for pharmacovigilance reporting.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: All 99 static analyzer findings across 9 files were evaluated and confirmed as false positives. The 'Weak cryptographic algorithm' detections are erroneous — the skill contains zero cryptographic code; the analyzer misidentified medical terminology strings. The 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' detections are from standard markdown code block fences (```) used for formatting in SKILL.md and reference files. The 'System reconnaissance' detections are from legitimate datetime formatting and stderr error reporting. The one 'Hardcoded URL' is the skill's own GitHub source link. The one 'filesystem' detection is standard CLI file output from user-specified arguments. The skill is a legitimate medical narrative generation tool with Python scripts that read JSON input and write text output — all documented and expected behavior for a CLI-based pharmacovigilance documentation tool. No prompt injection attempts, data exfiltration, or malicious behavior found. Risk factors (external_commands, filesystem, network) reflect legitimate CLI tool operation, not security threats.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL (Source Attribution Link)
low_findings.description: SKILL.md line 7 contains a hardcoded URL pointing to the skill's own GitHub source repository. This is a standard source attribution link, not a malicious or data-exfiltration URL. No sensitive data is transmitted to this URL. Risk is negligible.

dangerous_patterns.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm
dangerous_patterns.description: Static analyzer flagged 46 locations as weak cryptographic algorithm use. All are false positives. The skill contains no cryptographic code — it is a narrative text generator. The analyzer misidentified medical terminology strings, date format characters, and common words (e.g., 'MD' in MedDRA, hash-like patterns in sample IDs). Files affected: scripts/main.py, SKILL.md, references/CIOMS_I_Guidelines.md, references/ICSR_Template.md, references/Quick_Reference.md, references/MedDRA_Reference.md, references/sample_case_001.json, adverse-event-narrative_audit_result_v2.json.

dangerous_patterns.title: False Positive: Ruby/Shell Backtick Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: Static analyzer flagged backtick characters in markdown files as shell command execution. All are false positives. These are standard markdown code block fences (```) used for formatting code examples in SKILL.md and reference documentation files. No actual shell or Ruby backtick execution exists. Affected files: SKILL.md, references/ICSR_Template.md, references/Quick_Reference.md.

dangerous_patterns.title: False Positive: System/Network Reconnaissance
dangerous_patterns.description: Static analyzer flagged legitimate Python operations as reconnaissance. All are false positives. Flagged code includes: datetime.now().strftime() for generating report timestamps, print() to sys.stderr for error reporting, and standard dictionary access patterns. No actual system enumeration, network scanning, or information gathering occurs. Affected files: scripts/main.py, SKILL.md, references/CIOMS_I_Guidelines.md, references/Quick_Reference.md, references/MedDRA_Reference.md, references/sample_case_001.json.

dangerous_patterns.title: False Positive: Python File Write (Legitimate CLI Output)
dangerous_patterns.description: Static analyzer flagged open() with 'w' mode at scripts/main.py:364 as a file write risk. This is legitimate CLI behavior — the script writes generated narrative text to a user-specified output file path provided via command-line argument (--output). This is standard and expected for a document generation tool. No arbitrary file writes or path traversal.

content.value_statement: Writing regulatory-compliant adverse event narratives for ICSR submissions is time-consuming and error-prone when done manually. This skill automates narrative generation from structured case data following CIOMS I, ICH E2B, and FDA MedWatch standards, producing consistent draft narratives ready for physician review.
actual_capabilities: Generate CIOMS I-compliant adverse event narratives from structured JSON case data
Produce standardized ICSR narrative sections including patient demographics, suspect drugs, adverse events, dechallenge/rechallenge, outcome, and causality assessment
Validate case data completeness before narrative generation, identifying missing required fields
Support temporal relationship analysis with time-to-onset, dechallenge, and rechallenge assessment
Output narratives to file or standard output for integration with pharmacovigilance reporting workflows
Process clinical data with proper medical terminology handling including MedDRA Preferred Terms
limitations: Generates draft narratives only; all outputs require review by qualified drug safety physicians before regulatory submission
Does not perform independent causality assessment; it structures the reporter's evaluation rather than forming new clinical judgments
Optimized for English language narratives; other languages may require translation and manual adaptation
Individual case narratives only; aggregate signal detection and statistical analysis require separate tools

use_cases.title: Individual Case Safety Report Generation
use_cases.description: Drug safety professionals convert adverse event case data into CIOMS-compliant narratives for expedited and periodic regulatory submissions to health authorities.

use_cases.title: Clinical Trial Adverse Event Documentation
use_cases.description: Clinical researchers generate standardized adverse event narratives from trial safety data for inclusion in clinical study reports and investigator brochures.

use_cases.title: Periodic Safety Update Report Preparation
use_cases.description: Medical writers batch-process accumulated case data through the CIOMS narrative template to prepare the narrative sections of periodic safety update reports (PSURs) and periodic benefit-risk evaluation reports (PBRERs).

prompt_templates.title: Generate a single adverse event narrative
prompt_templates.prompt: Using the adverse-event-narrative skill, generate a CIOMS-compliant narrative from the case data in references/sample_case_001.json. Save the output to a text file named ICSR_narrative.txt.
prompt_templates.scenario: New user learning the basic narrative generation workflow with sample data

prompt_templates.title: Validate case data before narrative generation
prompt_templates.prompt: Run the adverse-event-narrative validator on my case data JSON file using --validate-only mode. Report any missing required fields or data quality issues before proceeding to narrative generation.
prompt_templates.scenario: Quality assurance check to ensure case data completeness before regulatory submission preparation

prompt_templates.title: Generate batch narratives for periodic reporting
prompt_templates.prompt: Using the adverse-event-narrative skill, process all case JSON files in the input directory. Generate individual CIOMS-compliant narratives for each case and compile them into a single report document with a summary table listing case IDs, events, outcomes, and causality assessments.
prompt_templates.scenario: Pharmacovigilance professional preparing quarterly periodic safety update report

prompt_templates.title: Custom narrative with temporal analysis and causality rationale
prompt_templates.prompt: Using the adverse-event-narrative skill, generate a comprehensive narrative from my case data using CIOMS I format. Include detailed temporal analysis with time-to-onset calculation, dechallenge and rechallenge assessment, WHO-UMC causality category with full rationale, and reference ranges for all diagnostic test values. Flag any data gaps that need medical reviewer attention before submission.
prompt_templates.scenario: Experienced pharmacovigilance professional preparing a complex expedited ICSR submission

output_examples.input: Case data for a 58-year-old female with lactic acidosis 13 days after starting metformin, with lab values showing elevated lactate and metabolic acidosis
output_examples.output:
CASE IDENTIFIER: 2024-ICSR-EX-001
DATE OF REPORT: 2024-02-15

PATIENT DEMOGRAPHICS:
A 58-year-old female. Physical characteristics: weight 65 kg, height 165 cm.

RELEVANT MEDICAL HISTORY:
- Hypertension (diagnosed 2019)
- Type 2 diabetes mellitus (diagnosed 2020)

CONCOMITANT MEDICATIONS:
- Lisinopril for Hypertension, 10 mg once daily
- Atorvastatin for Dyslipidemia, 20 mg once daily

SUSPECT DRUG(S):
Drug: Metformin (indicated for Type 2 diabetes mellitus)
  Dosage: 1000 mg twice daily via oral
  Therapy start: 2024-01-15
  Therapy stop: 2024-02-01

ADVERSE EVENT(S):
1. Lactic acidosis
   Onset date: 2024-01-28
   Time to onset: 13 days after first dose
   Severity: Severe
   Seriousness criteria: Hospitalization

DIAGNOSTIC TESTS AND RESULTS:
- Serum lactate: 8.5 mmol/L (reference: 0.5-2.2 mmol/L) on 2024-01-29
- Arterial blood gas pH: 7.25 (reference: 7.35-7.45) on 2024-01-29

DECHALLENGE AND RECHALLENGE:
Dechallenge: Positive - lactate normalized within 48 hours of discontinuation
Rechallenge: Not performed

OUTCOME: Recovered with sequelae
CAUSALITY ASSESSMENT: Probable

output_examples.input: Minimal case data with only required fields for a 45-year-old male with headache after starting a study drug
output_examples.output:
CASE IDENTIFIER: MIN-2024-001

PATIENT DEMOGRAPHICS:
A 45-year-old male.

SUSPECT DRUG(S):
Drug: Study Drug X (indicated for Hypertension)
  Therapy start: 2024-03-01

ADVERSE EVENT(S):
1. Headache
   Onset date: 2024-03-03

OUTCOME: Recovered

best_practices: Always validate case data with --validate-only before generating the full narrative to catch missing required fields and data quality issues early
Review every generated narrative against the CIOMS compliance checklist to confirm all required sections are present and accurate before submission
Have a qualified drug safety physician review and approve all narratives before regulatory submission, especially the causality assessment section
anti_patterns: Do not submit generated narratives without medical review; the skill produces drafts that require physician verification and approval
Do not include patient identifiers in case data; ensure all personal health information is de-identified before processing through the narrative generator
Do not rely on the skill for independent causality determination; it structures the reporter's assessment rather than forming a new independent clinical judgment

faq.question: What regulatory standards does this skill follow?
faq.answer: The skill follows CIOMS I guidelines for international adverse event reporting and supports ICH E2B(R3) electronic submission format for FDA, EMA, and other health authority submissions.

faq.question: Can this skill replace medical review of adverse event narratives?
faq.answer: No. The skill generates draft narratives to improve efficiency. All narratives require review and approval by qualified drug safety physicians before regulatory submission.

faq.question: What input format does the skill require?
faq.answer: The skill accepts JSON case data with required fields including case ID, patient demographics (age and sex), suspect drugs with dosing information, and adverse events with MedDRA terms.

faq.question: Can I use this skill for batch processing multiple cases?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill supports batch narrative generation for processing multiple case files, which is useful for periodic safety update reports and aggregate submissions.

faq.question: Does the skill integrate with MedDRA terminology?
faq.answer: The skill structures narratives to include MedDRA Preferred Terms for adverse events. It uses the MedDRA version installed in your environment for terminology accuracy.

faq.question: What should I do if the script reports validation errors?
faq.answer: Review each validation error message to identify missing required fields. Add the missing data to your case JSON file and re-run validation before generating the narrative.
